data.title: ByteTree Q4 2025 Quarterly Investor Letter
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome ByteTree Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Since 2013, our mission has been to help people invest better. We do this by delivering actionable, high-quality, contemporary investment guidance at an affordable price. With all the information out there, we help investors by distilling our advice into model portfolios, explaining every new investment recommendation in real time.

In this Q4 2025 update, I shall review the model portfolios, named Whisky and Soda, which I have been managing since January 2016. These are covered in The Multi-Asset Investor, which is published on Tuesdays. I shall also touch on some of our other services.

Letter from Charlie Morris, CEO

In 2025, stock markets were once again strong, but unlike recent years, the USA was left behind, returning 9.2%. The rest of the world fared better, with Europe returning 26%, the UK 25.8%, emerging markets 24.3%, and Japan 16%.

Global Equities, Gold, and Bitcoin in 2025

Currency movements against the weak dollar explain much of that performance as the Euro rose 13.4% (vs dollar), the Pound 7.6%, the Renminbi 4.4%, and the “weak” Yen 0.3%. It was the dollar that had a weak year.

A weak dollar was a tailwind for stock markets and, above all, the gold price, which beat all major equity markets. Yet Bitcoin, which is normally inversely sensitive to the dollar as well, fell by 13.1%. Gold’s strength was primarily driven by geopolitical concerns as the People’s Bank of China bought record amounts as they seek to hold neutral reserve assets. Bitcoin’s weakness was a late affair as the price cooled in Q4 alongside the pullback in technology and high-volatility stocks.

Bond yields fell in the UK after a sticky period over fiscal deficits, but lower inflation won the day, aided by a low oil price. It seems that Trump’s strategy is to run the economy hot, while keeping inflation contained via cheap energy. This partly explains his actions in Venezuela and the eagerness for increased production. Bond yields in the USA fell even faster. In Europe, they rose from low levels, but the big moves came from Asia. Chinese yields have been falling while Japanese yields have been rising at the fastest pace in years, as they normalise their bond market.

Global 10-Year Bond Yields

I want to take this opportunity to highlight the scale of the bubble in US equities. Many say the high returns are justified because the technology revolution is real, and US companies dominate it. That is probably true, but my concern is that high valuations lead to lower future returns. I am less concerned with the revolution in technology and AI, which will no doubt lead to worldwide improvements in productivity, but rather more with the impact on investors’ returns. The gap between the US and the rest of the world has been extreme.

Global Stock markets since 2011

US Equities CAPE Ratio

Source: Shiller Data

Shiller plots the “excess CAPE Yield” in blue below. That is the earnings yield (CAPE inverted) less the real 10-year bond yield. This shows investors how much “excess return” they can expect from equities over the inflation-adjusted risk-free rate available from government bonds. This was high in 1921, in 1932 after the great Wall Street crash, and in 1982 after a painful period in markets during the 1970s. The excess CAPE yield was low in 1929 before the crash, in 1970 ahead of that rough decade, and in 2000 at the peak of the dotcom bubble.

Excess CAPE Yield (ECY) and Subsequent 10-Year Annualised Excess Returns

Source: Shiller Data

The green line shows the subsequent 10-year rate of return as it actually happened and therefore is set back by ten years. In 2025, we now know that in 2015, the stock market returned 14% of excess returns over bonds for the next ten years, but we didn’t know that then. The power of this chart is how accurate it has been in the past for anticipating future “expected returns”. With an excess CAPE yield of 1.5%, we can reasonably expect the S&P 500 to do very poorly over the next decade.

A similar idea was published by the fund manager GMO. They use current pricing models to anticipate returns over the next seven years by asset class. They also split out the forecast by interest rate environment, whereby low rates will boost equity returns and vice versa. They favour small caps, especially in Japan and emerging markets. Above all, they favour deep value, which is what I focus on, and this is why. They are cautious on both US large- and small-caps. As for bonds, they expect to see positive real returns, which contrasts with this dataset published five years ago, which showed negative expected returns. That proved to be correct.

GMO: 7-Year Real Return Forecasts

I highlight this long-term thinking because it is important that investors understand it. The US market may fly for a few more years, or it may slump; nobody knows. But over a longer time horizon, it is very likely that non-US equities beat US equities, just as they did after the dotcom crisis for a decade.

Finally, you cannot ignore how tough these times are for consumers. Current readings are in line with the 1980 recession, the financial crisis, and the pandemic. Stocks have risen while the consumer has remained under pressure, in what some call a K-shaped recovery.

The ByteTree Money Map

It is an investment process I have been refining for many years, too many perhaps. Some years are good, others less so, but I am always consistent in my approach, finding opportunities backed by fundamental value.

The Multi-Asset Investor

The Multi-Asset Investor is a commentary and two portfolios, called Whisky and Soda, that cover traditional assets. The portfolios are designed to be easy to replicate for investors managing their own money. Soda is low turnover and medium risk. Whisky is more dynamic and medium-high risk. It is written by Charlie Morris and updated weekly, with additional Flash Notes when immediate action is required.

The Whisky Portfolio

It has been a strong quarter for the Whisky Portfolio, which was up 39.0% in 2025, against the FTSE 100, which was up a respectable 25.8%. Notably, the FTSE 100 is now above 10,000 for the first time in its history. Since 2016, that has been the best nominal return for Whisky, but not the best outperformance, which was reserved for 2020, when Whisky delivered a good return against a weak market.

Whisky vs FTSE 100

Performance picked up after 2020 for two main reasons. The first is surely that I dedicated myself to this full-time after I left the city. The second is that I started to embrace more European and global stocks, despite the portfolio remaining predominantly in the UK. In 2025, you could add high exposure to precious metals and the mining stocks.

Whisky Portfolio Performance

Barry Callebaut, the Swiss chocolatier, was the most enjoyable note to write in Q4. It was an interesting situation, and a high-quality stock that had been shaken by the surge in cocoa futures. Cocoa prices are now down over 50% from their peak, as reports of better yields and improving disease rates increase. The company has taken steps to insulate itself from a recurrence and is recovering as the cocoa market normalises.

Malaysia was added too, a fast-growing country that has been overlooked by investors. The market was trading at a low valuation despite a strong currency over two decades, and with a cost of borrowing below that of the UK.

PHP, the healthcare REIT, was another good find. It has stable tenants in the healthcare sector, including hospitals, doctors, and dentists, has a strong balance sheet, and pays a 7% yield even after the gains since being added. Whisky judges value on its merit, and is happy to buy it in whatever form, wherever it comes from.

The Soda Portfolio

Soda is up 19.9% this year against the FTSE Private Balanced Index, which is up 11.7%, meaning 4.5% of outperformance. The previous two years were tough, mainly because market returns were driven by the large tech stocks, and I refused to buy them. More fool me, perhaps, but I stick to my investment process where I can find tangible value. I believe that big tech will grind to a halt in terms of delivering outsized returns, and that will hopefully provide a healthy tailwind of relative performance over the coming years.

Soda vs FTSE Private Balanced Index

Soda has returned 155.5% since inception against 117.2% for the benchmark, which translates into a rate of return of 9.8% against 8.1% for the balanced index. Notably, Soda’s volatility remains materially lower than the index, which demonstrates effective diversification. The Sharpe Ratio remains much higher than the index, and I am pleased with that.

The long-term level of outperformance has been less impressive than in Soda, but ironically, this “balanced” index has a much higher exposure to technology stocks than the FTSE, which has none. I am confident that the gap, which shows relative performance, will continue to widen over the long term, especially should US equities lag the rest of the world.

Soda Portfolio Performance

Last year, Soda increased exposure to RIT Capital (RCP), which got a boost from its holding in SpaceX, which is expected to IPO this year with a trillion-dollar price tag. This is proof that balanced portfolios can still be interesting.

The portfolio took profits in European banks, which had been fabulous. The normalisation of the yield curve made banking highly profitable again, following years of zero rates. Perhaps that was too soon, but other new investments, such as Swiss equities and REITs, seemed compelling.

The BlackRock Mining Trust (BRWM) was a notable performer in 2026, returning 67%, as the mining stocks had their best run since 2016.

Japan remains a high-conviction overweight. The normalisation of the bond market, with a cheap currency, should lead to strong performance in the long term. Japanese equities have been the worst place to invest since 1990, and this should change.

I also highlight BH Macro [BHMG](BHMDF), which has done a good job over the years, delivering healthy returns when things go wrong. We bought in at a discount in 2024, and while not much has happened for them in 2025, it is worth remembering what that can add to a portfolio when it matters.

The ByteTree Quality Portfolio

The ByteTree Quality service was launched on 24 August 2025, and we started building the portfolio with our first stock, Diageo (DEO), on 3 September. Diageo’s multi-year downtrend continues for now, although it briefly jumped on the news that Dave Lewis, famous at Tesco for driving operational excellence, had been named the new CEO. He is just what the company needs. Meanwhile, late 2025 saw signs of a shift in the narrative about declining drinking habits, with full pubs for Christmas and falling Dry January commitment among younger consumers. Diageo is holding the portfolio back for now, but over any reasonable time frame, we remain confident in its outlook.

In December, we unlocked our second recommendation, Nestlé (NSRGY), which surged following the new CEO’s announcement of major layoffs and a “turnaround fire”. It has the brands to succeed long-term, and given the price paid, its prospects are excellent. Healthcare was also a dominant source of additions to the portfolio in the quarter, as the sector showed a balance of value and momentum that is rare in today’s highly valued markets.

We investigated and rejected a number of companies too, either on valuation or resilience grounds. Our focus is on the highest quality companies, with very high conviction in their future performance, which could reasonably be held for decades. Many great companies do not qualify, as uncertainty and the threat of disruption are too great. The discipline of what not to invest in is just as important as what we do invest in, especially given the current state of financial markets.

With 11 stocks recommended to date and an additional spinoff, we aim to have around 24 stocks by the spring. Thereafter, the portfolio will continue to identify new opportunities when suitable companies are offered at low prices, with a positive outlook. The idea is that the turnover will be low but remain active. A robust group of stocks that should perform well over the long term, while proving resilient during times of stress. It is a different way of doing things that should appeal to long-term investors who take comfort in holding resilient businesses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WEBTOON Entertainment: The Story Is Still Writing Itself
data.description:
I still think WEBTOON Entertainment (WBTN) is one of the most interesting subscription services for consumers that I've looked into. It is at the crossroads of the creative economy, mobile entertainment, and IP monetization. Its business strategy combines recurring Paid Content revenue with high-upside Advertising and IP Adaptations. That's why I started covering it in August 2024 with a Buy rating and a $16 price tag.

Since then, the basics have changed in a way that matters. Global MAUs have dropped to about 168 million (down about 8.5% from last year), Korea has gotten a lot weaker, and management's profit trajectory has changed. Advertising has become cyclical, dropping 8.9% YoY (FX-neutral) in 3Q25. IP revenue, on the other hand, has become very uneven, soaring +171.8% YoY (FX-neutral) in 3Q25 but being hard to anticipate from one quarter to the next. Profitability is now the most important factor: Adjusted EBITDA dropped from $28.9 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $5.1 million in the third quarter of 2025. The guidance for the fourth quarter of 2025 suggests an EBITDA loss of about $1.5 million to $6.5 million.

At the stock level, WBTN has shown that its visibility profile has changed by trading in a wide range of about $6.75 to $22.47 during the past 52 weeks. The platform is still strategically useful, and Japan is still doing well, but the business is less straightforward than I thought it would be after the IPO. In this update, I'll go over what's new, compare my initial thesis to the data, and explain why I'm lowering WBTN's rating to a Hold.

Activity and Making Money on the Platform

WEBTOON's total number of monthly active users (MAUs) around the world dropped to about 168 million, which is about 8.5% less than last year. The number of app MAUs fell by 4.2% from last year, mostly outside of Japan and English-speaking areas. The number of users in Korea dropped to about 24.6 million MAUs, a 12.3% drop from the previous year. Partially making up for that, Japan's MAUs rose to about 25.3 million, a 12.6% increase from the previous year. English language app MAUs also rose by about 12% from the previous year.

So the platform went from having a lot of global MAU growth to a profile where Japan and English-speaking markets are driving more of the growth, while Korea and Wattpad-related areas are under pressure. Importantly, the depth of monetization got better in the markets that stayed strong. Japan's ARPPU rose to about $23.60, which is about 1.3% more than last year when the exchange rate was stable. Because of this, WEBTOON has to rely more and more on greater ARPPU and payer penetration to make up for a smaller total viewership in some areas.

To put it another way, the number of users went down, but the number of users who could be turned into money went up in important areas.

Mix of Income

Paid Content remained to be the model's mainstay, thanks to better ARPPU and MPU conversion rates in Japan and English-speaking areas. At the same time, Advertising and IP Adaptations, the two business lines that were supposed to bring in more money, turned out to be far more cyclical and timing-based.

On an FX-neutral basis, advertising revenue fell 8.9% year over year in 3Q25. The main reason for this shortcoming was low expenditure on online ads in Korea and uneven demand around the world. So, earlier hopes that Advertising would grow significantly at scale were changed to show a more cyclical revenue foundation with less leverage in the near term. IP revenue went in the opposite way, but it was just as volatile. In the third quarter of 2025, IP revenue grew by 171.8% year over year, not taking into account changes in currency values. This was due to the timing of some project releases. But this strength wasn't the same every quarter. In IP, revenue recognition depended on when adaptations reached monetization milestones. This made it considerably harder to anticipate quarterly outcomes. The total impact was that WEBTOON's top-line growth was greatly affected by how Paid Content monetization trends worked with two other revenue sources that were significantly more unstable.

Reset of Profitability

Expectations for profitability altered a lot. Adjusted EBITDA went down from $28.9 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $5.1 million in the third quarter of 2025. This meant that operating leverage dropped sharply because advertising didn't grow and IP stayed uneven. The business's guidance for 4Q25 said that its adjusted EBITDA loss would be between $1.5 million and $6.5 million. This further shows that the company is currently in a period of pressured margins instead of stable margin growth.

It was thought that WEBTOON might steadily climb toward low- to mid-teens EBITDA margins over time, but this is now less certain in the short term.

Summary of the region

Korea's MAUs fell to about 24.6 million, a drop of 12.3% from the previous year. The main problem was that advertising wasn't doing well because of Korean e-commerce spending. Paid content did better, but not well enough to make up for the pressure from ads.

Japan's MAUs grew 12.6% year over year to over 25.3 million. ARPPU went up 1.3% YoY, or about $23.60, when the exchange rate was not taken into account. Japan was still the best example of how WEBTOON's fundamental model works, with both audience growth and monetization depth going up.

Areas where English is spoken

The number of monthly active users (MAUs) for the app went up about 12% year over year. New partnerships with creators and cross-promotion of intellectual property helped this area grow.

The total number of monthly active users (MAUs) worldwide fell by about 8.5% year over year to over 168 million. App MAUs fell by 4.2% from last year. Since the IPO, one of the most important data trends has been the change in the mix toward Japan and English-speaking markets and away from Korea and Wattpad-sensitive areas.

The context of stock performance

The stock price has changed to show this changing visibility profile. Over the last 52 weeks, the price of shares has ranged from about $6.75 to $22.47. The peaks in the range matched up with times when IP announcements and feelings about Western expansion were stronger. There were lows surrounding earnings reports as advertising trends got weaker and EBITDA estimates were adjusted.

Investors are starting to see WEBTOON as a platform with a strong long-term position, but with short-term changes in both MAUs and profits guiding Tone and Market Perception Management, changed their approach to guiding, focusing on disciplined expenditure and making revenue visible instead of aggressive growth goals. Some of the revenue estimates included situations where FX-neutral growth was in the negative low single digits, mostly because of weak advertising and timing issues with IP.

Street projections followed this change in tone. Expectations for EBITDA in 2025 and 2026E were lowered compared to models made right after the IPO.

How the Business Looks Today vs One Year Ago

  • More money made per user in Japan and English-speaking areas
  • Fewer global MAUs
  • Advertising revenue down 8.9% YoY FX stable in 3Q25
  • IP revenue is up 171.8% YoY FX is stable in 3Q25, but not always
  • Adjusted EBITDA dropped from $28.9M to $5.1M YoY in 3Q
  • 4Q25 forecast suggests that EBITDA might lose between $1.5M and $6.5M
  • The stock traded between $6.75 and $22.47 over the time

In short, Paid Content is still strong, Japan is doing well, and English markets are rising, but Advertising softness and IP lumpiness have made it harder to predict profits and losses.

My New Opinion on Webtoon

My main argument in August 2024 was based on four main ideas:

(1) WEBTOON as a unique storytelling platform with strong protective features

(2) Japan and Korea as major drivers of growth

(3) Advertising and IP Adaptations as the second part of the story

(4) Long-term operating leverage resulting in steady EBITDA growth

I want to go over each pillar one at a time and grade my own thesis objectively now that I have more information. I don't want to "sell a story" here; I just want to show you how my thinking has changed now that we have more information.

1. Quality of the platform and moat

What I thought

I thought WEBTOON's platform had a structural advantage because of:

  • 160M+ MAUs
  • high engagement
  • a sticky creator community
  • powerful monetization mechanisms

I thought WEBTOON was the best platform for serialized mobile storytelling, and I thought it would become better over time as it grew.

What took place

I still think the platform is the best in its class, but the moat today seems more dependent on execution than on structure.

Since I wrote my post, the number of global MAUs has dropped by about 8.5% year over year, to about 168 million. There were 4.2% less MAUs on apps this year than last year. Korea's population dropped 12.3% from last year to about 24.6 million. Wattpad had an effect on regions that got weaker. The offset came from the fact that Japan's MAUs grew by 12.6% YoY to about 25.3 million and English app MAUs grew by about 12% YoY.

So, people are still using the site a lot, but it is not immune to competition or changes in the economy. The user base is stronger in Japan and English-speaking countries but weaker in other places.

What I think currently

I think the moat is there, but you can't just "set it and forget it." You need to keep putting money into user growth, creator monetization, and the content pipeline. The moat gets smaller if that investment slows down or advertising weakens.

So I still think the platform quality is good, but I'm not sure if I can call it a strong moat without more information.

2. Japan and Korea Are Important Places for Growth

What I thought

  • Japan would become a growth engine for many years
  • Korea would stay strong even as it got older
  • Payer conversion and ARPPU would go up

What happened?

Japan came through. MAUs gained 12.6% year over year to around 25.3 million, and ARPPU rose to about $23.60, or about 1.3% year over year, not excluding currency fluctuations. As the number of users grew, monetization got better, which is what I thought would happen. Korea did not.

MAUs fell by 12.3% from the previous year to about 24.6 million. Weakness in advertising linked to e-commerce spending was a major problem. Paid content did okay, but not well enough to make up for the drop in ads caused by the economy.

Japan still backs the WEBTOON model. Korea proves that even the best markets may have ups and downs. I think the story in the area changed from "broad-based Asian strength" to "Japan-led stability with some softness in other places."

That's not bad, but it's not what I thought it would be.

3. Using Advertising and IP Adaptations to Speed Up Growth

What I thought

  • Advertising would grow rapidly and steadily
  • IP adaptations would become a significant source of income
  • Both lines would increase exposure and variety

What took place

Advertising stopped building on itself and started going in cycles.

In the third quarter of 2025, ad revenue fell 8.9% YoY FX neutral. This was mostly because Korean e-commerce was weak. The business didn't fail, but the growth path I was hoping for slowed down.

IP changes got very strong, but they were also uneven.

IP revenue went up 171.8% year over year in 3Q25, not counting currency fluctuations. But the growth was caused by when projects were released. It is challenging to make predictions or annualize with confidence.

So instead of two stabilizers, we got two things that made everything more volatile.

Where I stand now

I still think both sources of income are important for strategy.

But I don't think Advertising will keep growing smoothly or give me operating leverage every quarter anymore. Also, I don't think of IP as linear. Paid Content is still the most important thing to me.

IP and advertising are crucial, but time and execution are even more important than I thought.

4. Increasing Margins and Using Operating Leverage

What I thought

I thought I would see an increase in EBITDA margin over time, thanks to:

  • A greater advertising mix
  • More efficient content
  • Worldwide scale

I thought it would be possible to get mid-teens EBITDA over time.

What took place

  • Adjusted EBITDA fell substantially from the previous year.
  • EBITDA for the third quarter of 2024: $28.9 million
  • EBITDA for the third quarter of 2025: $5.1 million
  • Then, guidance meant 4Q25 EBITDA: a loss of about $1.5 million to $6.5 million

This shows me that operating leverage can work in both directions. When advertising slows down and IP timing changes, fixed costs become important.

How I feel right now

The long-term model might still get to double-digit margins, but the route is more unpredictable and less straight than I thought at first.

I think that WEBTOON is in "margin reset" mode, not "margin expansion" mode.

Also, it's harder to see.

How I would sum up my new thesis

What still works from my original thesis:

  • WEBTOON is still the best in its category
  • Japan is still becoming stronger
  • Paid Content monetization is real
  • The creator ecosystem is still very important

What has changed

  • MAUs are not immune to massive
  • Advertising goes in cycles
  • IP is strong but depends on timing
  • EBITDA leverage isn't always there in the short run

My neutral opinion

I think WEBTOON is still a unique digital storytelling platform with a good chance of lasting for a long time. Japan and English-speaking areas show that monetization depth can increase as the number of users grows. The creator ecosystem is still a competitive edge.

But at the same time, the number of worldwide MAUs fell by about 8.5% year over year, and advertising income fell by 8.9%. Year over year, FX was stable in 3Q25, but EBITDA fell from $28.9M to $5.1M YoY, and the 4Q25 projection suggests possible losses.

Today, I see a healthier long-term asset, but I also see poorer profits visibility and more cyclical revenue dynamics than I thought I would after the IPO.

That balance makes me neutral for now.

  • Paid content works.
  • Japan is working.
  • Execution and macro are more important than I thought.

As such, I update my investment recommendation to hold/neutral with a price target equal to today's prices, as I believe it's fairly priced today.

Dear Partners,

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the fund generated a return of 13.65% gross of fees and 10.85% net of fees, compared to 2.33% for the S&P 500 Total Return Index and 3.29% for our MSCI ACWI Total Return benchmark. For the full year 2025, the fund generated a return of 52.04% gross and 45.60% net of fees, significantly outperforming the S&P 500's 16.26% and the MSCI ACWI’s 22.34%. The market experienced notable volatility over the past quarter yet ultimately finished near another record high to end the year. We exited several positions as they either reached our target prices or experienced structural fundamental changes, and initiated new investments aligned with the ongoing “China vs. U.S. tension” theme. While we expect this narrative to remain a key driver in 2026, we hold precious metals and hedge positions to protect the portfolio against inflation and potential economic setbacks.

Market Commentary

Market concerns about the AI capex cycle intensified this past quarter, beginning with Oracle reporting results that fell short of expectations. This led to a period of heightened volatility, which later stabilized as the Fed lowered interest rates and expanded its balance sheet, while Japan raised interest rates to draw capital back to the U.S. markets.

Looking ahead to 2026, we expect the “U.S. vs China” race to remain the dominant theme. China still lacks the ability to design and manufacture leading-edge, high-performance chips at the level of the U.S. and its allies. Meanwhile, the U.S. continues to face bottlenecks in domestic manufacturing capacity for key components and supply of critical materials, alongside slower-than-needed progress in energy infrastructure build-out and grid upgrade. National security has also become a central focus for both nations. Against this backdrop, technology velocity and government policy execution will decide the leader in this race. We will continue to monitor the development closely and search for opportunities with our eyes wide open.

Portfolio Update

We ended the quarter with approximately 35 percent in gold and cash.

Cyclical/Secular Gainers

The Tension Basket

We continue to add ideas to our tension basket to capture opportunities arising from the U.S. vs. China theme. At year-end, our holdings included Vicor (VICR) (VICR.NASDAQ), whose industry-leading power solutions are poised to benefit from data center architecture’s transition to HVDC yet remain under-covered and misunderstood; ACM Research (ACMR) (ACMR.NASDAQ), China’s leading wafer-fab cleaning equipment manufacturer, positioned to benefit from China’s push toward semiconductor self-sufficiency; Lumentum (LITE) (LITE.NASDAQ), a market leader in optical and photonic products whose OCS and optics solutions are positioned to benefit from strong TPU demand and continued XPU evolution; and two Taiwanese companies, including a semiconductor cooling solution provider and a high-precision PCB drilling solution provider. We also added Huntington Ingalls (HII) (HII.NYSE) to capture the need for increased shipbuilding as defense becomes another key battleground between the rivals and government spending focuses on naval readiness.

Vicor (VICR.NASDAQ)

Over the past quarter, we added Vicor, a leading U.S. designer and manufacturer of high-performance power modules, as a core position. Vicor is positioned to benefit from rising AI-driven power requirements and the transition toward HVDC architectures. Despite this, Vicor remains a misunderstood small-cap company with limited Wall Street coverage. We believe company guidance and street consensus are way too conservative and Vicor is a multi-bagger set to capture a multi-year tailwind.

Vicor is the gold standard in “last-inch” 48V-to-sub-1V vertical power delivery, enabling high-current conversion near the XPU to minimize I²R losses, heat, and EMI while sustaining high efficiency. Vicor’s differentiated architecture and industry-leading technology have enabled long-standing relationships with key AI players. In the second half of 2025, Vicor has completed qualification for a lead customer’s next- generation platform, which we believe to be Nvidia, and we believe Google is likely to follow suit, along with two additional tier-one OEMs, as management has disclosed. We believe Vicor is on the cusp of adding more customers as the industry leader in the last mile HVDC power delivery. These programs represent discrete, multi-year revenue opportunities that have yet to be reflected in consensus expectations.

Beyond revenue upside, Vicor offers meaningful margin expansion opportunity. Historically, the company’s hardware business has generated 40–50% gross margins. As the newly ramped facility comes online, we expect hardware margins post vertical integration to move materially higher, as the capacity is purpose-built for high-end applications like AI and aerospace. Additionally, Vicor is actively scaling its IP licensing business, which has already reached a meaningful run rate despite monetizing only a narrow, largely legacy subset of its patent portfolio and engaging with a limited number of large customers. Management is guiding toward a $200 million annual licensing run rate by 2027 to 2028, which we view as conservative given the depth of unmonetized IP (multi-billion-dollar TAM) and early traction ($90M annual run rate as of 3Q:25). We believe the licensing business, combined with vertical integration and next-generation high-end hardware products, will meaningfully shift the revenue mix and position Vicor for significant margin expansion.

Taken together, customer wins, expanded production capacity, and IP monetization make Vicor’s long- term targets of $1B in revenue and 65% corporate margins appear conservative. With street consensus still well below these targets, we see a meaningful delta between market expectations and underlying fundamentals. As the thesis plays out and earnings scale, we believe Vicor presents a compelling Davis Double Play.

Closing

We would like to thank our partners for another year of support. While we had a solid year and our stock selection appears directionally correct so far, we believe our execution could have been better as we became more comfortable investing in momentum driven AI-related businesses in the second half of the year.

Our goal as investors has always been, and always will be, to become the best investors and partners we can be to honor our fiduciary duty. To achieve this, we will continue to evolve by refining our approach and adding new tools to our investment toolkit, rather than confining ourselves to a well-defined style box.

As we move away from investing solely through a traditional contrarian value lens and integrate a more holistic approach that places greater emphasis on market sentiment and the inevitability of long-term technological development, some may view this as a deviation from traditional value investing, but we see it as a natural progression of value thinking in the post Munger and Buffett era, especially in today’s market environment.

We are mindful of the momentum-driven nature of some of our holdings and expect higher portfolio turnover. As such, we will implement tighter stops and more disciplined risk management accordingly. Ultimately, our goal is to blend these disciplines and thrive across all market conditions as we explore whether growth, momentum, and value can coexist in a way that works.

This may mean we will not attract certain types of institutional clients, as most institutions prefer to select managers based on rigid style boxes. However, we believe that investing is more art than science. Howard Marks once said that in order to be great, one must be willing to be different. We dare to be great and will let that principle guide us. We intend to walk a path that cannot be easily imitated by others, whether human or artificial—our own path.

We appreciate your trust as you grow alongside us and witness our growth as investors.

Sincerely,

Vincent Lo, CFA, MBA

Chief Investment Officer & Partner Kathmandu Capital, LLC

data.title: MTU Aero Engines: Why It's Underperformed And The Market Misses The Bigger Picture
data.description:
MTU Aero Engines AG (MTUAY) (MTUAF) received a Strong Buy rating in my last report. However, the aerospace and defense stock remained flat, underperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) gain. In this report, I discuss the recent share price action, update my price target, and explain why I believe MTUAY stock remains a Strong Buy and the market is missing the bigger picture.

MTU Aero Engines Stock Decline Followed Aerospace Peers, Recovery Did Not

Looking at the price performance, we see that aerospace peers saw share price declines until the start of December. Generally, we have seen that the prospects of peace resulted in share price pressure for aerospace and defense companies. At the same time, from December onward, we saw that tension in the Caribbean was increasing, ultimately leading to the capture of Venezuelan leader Maduro. That is a regional pressure that benefits U.S. aerospace and defense stocks more than it would benefit MTU Aero Engines, which also is not a pure-play defense company.

On top of that, there was the interest rate decision from the Fed in December, which sparked an uptick in U.S. equities. As a European stock, MTU Aero Engines did not benefit in the same way. On top of that, we had two reductions in the interest rates in the U.S., which benefited the U.S. stock markets. U.S. aerospace and defense equities continued the rally following the news that President Trump targets a $1.5 trillion military budget, which again is likely to benefit U.S. aerospace and defense companies more than European companies.

Specific to MTU Aero Engines stock, some share price pressure was likely introduced by the issuance of €600 million in convertible bonds due in 2033. Investors can perceive this as dilution in disguise, but we note that the company also repurchased its Convertible Bonds due in 2027, so MTU Aero Engines uses the convertible bonds not to eventually and certainly dilute shareholders but to have a debt instrument with optionality.

MTU Aero Engines Remains Extremely Undervalued

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Price targets are based on the ordinary shares listed in Germany. Investors should keep in mind that the ticker MTUAF represents the ordinary shares, while the ticker MTUAY represents 0.5x ordinary shares.

For MTU Aero Engines, I maintain my Strong Buy rating with a base case price target of $544.25. We note that the stock price does not reflect earnings for 2025, and the company is undervalued against aerospace peers, while it is in a net cash position, which is definitely not something that many companies can say. Estimates on total EBITDA generation between 2025 and 2027 have improved by 1.8%, while the 2024-2027 CAGR has improved from 10.9% to 11.3%. For free cash flow, total generation estimates improved substantially by 10.2%, bringing the CAGR from 60.6% to 65.3%.

In terms of margins, we see little upside for MTU Aero Engines, but cash conversion rates are expected to improve substantially in the years ahead by around 100 basis points annually, driving operating cash performance and resulting in capital expenditures as a percentage of operating cash flow to reduce sharply from 69% in 2024 to 41% in 2027. Absent any acquisitions or shareholder returns beyond what is currently modelled, the company will see its net cash position widen, which is a positive, but at the same time I do believe the company has to either put that excess cash to work or return it to shareholders through share repurchases.

There Is A Lot To Like About MTU Aero Engines

From a fundamental perspective, I believe there is substantial upside for MTU Aero Engines that the market has failed to appreciate as it is squarely focused on the 32.7x P/E ratio, which is considered high by many even though aerospace and industry peers are currently trading at 43x P/E. Furthermore, I believe we should also highlight the appreciable business mix that MTU Aero Engine has. The company has exposure to military engines as well as commercial airplane engines, which are the main source of growth.

The commercial airplane portfolio includes 23 platforms, which consist of wide-body and single-aisle airplanes as well as mature engine platforms and new engine platforms:

  • GE9X for the Boeing 777X
  • CFM LEAP-1A/B for the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo
  • GTF engine family powering the Airbus A320neo family, Airbus A220, and Embraer E-2
  • IAE V2500 for the A320
  • CFM56-5B/7B for the Boeing 737 Next Generation and Airbus A320ceo
  • CF6 and GE90, which remain key engines for earlier-generation wide-body aircraft such as the Boeing 777 and Boeing 767

The military engine portfolio supports the following platforms:

  • Eurofighter Typhoon
  • Airbus A400M
  • Sikorsky CH-53K
  • F-16
  • F/A-18
  • F-15 / F-15EX
  • New Generation Fighter

The industrial product portfolio includes the LM6000 and LM2500 engines, which are derivatives of the CF6 aero engine. The company derives revenues from OEM, or original equipment manufacturer, sales, which are relatively lower-margin engine module sales to the engine manufacturers. Growing these engine module deliveries is key to the company’s aftermarket sales channel, which comes at a higher margin. So, the OEM sales are foundational to the true profit maker for MTU.

Conclusion: MTU Aero Engines Remains Attractive

MTU Aero Engines stock has not performed well since my last report, but I believe that is generally driven by MTU Aero Engines not having some of the benefits that U.S. equities had, and in particular U.S. aerospace and defense stocks. That, however, does not mean that MTU Aero Engines stock is not attractive. In fact, I believe that as a manufacturer of engine modules, the company has extremely bright prospects that are currently not being appreciated by the market.

data.title: Core CPI YoY Lowest Since March 2021, Stocks Record Highs, Key Charts
data.description:
The December 2025 CPI verified close to economists’ expectations, rising 0.3%. That brings the year-on-year rate to 2.7%, at estimates. Core CPI was cooler than expected, increasing by just 0.2%, holding the annual rate at 2.6%, which matches for the lowest since March 2021. It was the 58th straight month that the YoY CPI rate was above the Fed’s 2% target.

Carrying the numbers out an extra decimal place, the December headline CPI rose 0.31%, and core CPI increased 0.24%. Supercore CPI was +0.287%, taking the YoY rate to 2.741% (up from 2.703% in November). Real average hourly earnings are now +1.1% YoY, up from 0.8% in November.

Within the report, food prices climbed 0.7% from November, while energy CPI tallied a 0.3% sequential bump. Core goods inflation, key to tariff developments, was unchanged, helped by a 1.1% monthly dip in used vehicle prices. Apparel costs ticked up 0.6%, however.

Core services CPI added 0.3% last month, supported by a 0.4% shelter CPI rise and a 0.3% increase in rents. Hotel prices surged 3.5% in December, while vehicle insurance was a boon to commuters’ wallets, falling 0.4% in the year’s final month.

Equity futures initially rose, but then settled back once the numbers were digested. Treasury yields eased, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) continuing to fail at the 4.20% key technical levels. Gold, silver, and bitcoin were all positive in the premarket.

December CPI Slightly Cooler Than Expectations

U.S. Core CPI YoY Lowest Since March 2021

0% Core Goods Inflation in December

CPI & Core CPI M/M and Y/Y

Supercore CPI Rose In December vs. November

CPI Categories YoY

CPI Using Real-Time Shelter Price Data is Under 2%

U.S. Truflation is Low at 1.74%

The 10-year Treasury Yield Was Rejected at 4.20% Again

IWM Hit Fresh Records Pre-Market

The VIX Dipped Under 15 Post-CPI

Looking ahead, inflation data continues rolling in this week. December PPI hits tomorrow morning, with key components feeding into the PCE Price Index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Then tomorrow afternoon, the Fed’s Beige Book of regional business activity offers color on year-end sentiment and trends across the country.

Also keep your eye on upcoming January data points—the Philly Fed Index and the NY Empire Manufacturing Index are released at the same time as jobless claims Thursday morning. Industrial Production and the NAHB Housing Index arrive before the long weekend.

Data Calendar

U.S. Q4 GDP likely near 3.5%

The Bottom Line

Traders were not bracing for much CPI-related volatility. Indeed, the S&P 500 (SP500) was indicated unchanged after the data hit the tape, but small-cap Russell 2000 futures notched more record highs after the 8:30 a.m. ET report. I’m watching the 4.2% level on the 10-year Treasury, while bank earnings continue rolling in.

data.title: Celestica: When Engineering Becomes The Moat
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica Inc. (CLS) is not a low-margin contract assembler anymore but is instead a highly embedded engineering partner for the hyperscalers who develop AI data centers. This leadership position in high-bandwidth networking, fast time-to-production, and R&D-driven moat anchors a 28%+ revenue CAGR through 2028.

Since my first coverage, CLS is up 282%, and 26% since my last. With CCS programs scaling, 50%+ hyperscaler share, and a visible pipeline into 1.6T and 3.2T platforms, execution speed and incumbency make displacement increasingly unlikely.

Celestica Targets 28%+ Revenue CAGR Through 2028

Celestica’s primary bullish attribute is its expanding role as a high-end tech and design partner for hyperscale clients building AI data centers. There is a deeper integration that secures future top line and creates a competitive barrier. Celestica’s transformation began in 2018 when it was moving away from low-complexity programs. Celestica now focuses on high-performance data center tech. The results of this directional change are seen in the outlook and segment performance.

The Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment (I see this as the epicenter of this activity) may grow its top-line by ~40% in 2026, and CLS’s management sees a path for at least 40% growth continuing into 2027. This outlook is built on specific high-value programs. For instance, Celestica expects its High-Performance Solutions portfolio to derive ~$5 billion in topline for 2025 alone (an 80% growth YoY). This growth is tied to direct business engagements.

The technical side of these engagements forms Celestica’s upside. Celestica employs over 1,100 design engineers and is increasing its research spending by at least 50% in 2026, directed at next-gen tech for AI workloads like 800-gig and 1.6T Ethernet switches, rack-scale liquid cooling, and custom ASIC AI compute. Also, Celestica collaborated on a first-of-its-kind rack-scale liquid-cooled 1.6T networking product for a hyperscaler.

Celestica focuses on design engineering and its capability to operationalize complex production lines. As per the management, after receiving initial silicon samples, Celestica produced an operational prototype for the 1.6T switch in a matter of days, and it is a process that takes competitors weeks. This speed and technical expertise in next-gen hardware are advantages for top-line growth. Why? This technical lead translates directly into market share. In the high-bandwidth Ethernet switching industry, the total market share of Celestica’s offerings in the combined 200-gig, 400-gig, and 800-gig platforms stands at 41%.

In the market segment for customized products for hyperscalers, Celestica's share of total spending is 55%. This market lead in the highest-performance tiers is self-reinforcing. As tech cycles shorten and complexity increases (moving from 800G to 1.6T and beyond), incumbents, and new entrants may find it difficult to match Celestica’s accumulated expertise and existing client integration.

Moreover, the forward top-line pipeline appears solid based on awarded programs. The 2026 top-line forecast of $16 billion is a central growth point. It is a 31% increase from the 2025 outlook. Beyond that, a major program with a digital native client for a rack-scale custom AI system may begin mass production in 2027. As per the management, this single engagement is worth at least a few billion dollars in the first year. For me, the visibility extends further as management confirms Celestica has already won follow-on programs for next-gen silicon that ramp into 2027 and is working on 3.2T tech for 2028 and beyond.

Overall, this combination of deep engineering integration with large data center clients, a lead in next-gen AI networking tech, big market share in its niche, and a visible multi-year pipeline of awarded high-value projects forms a bullish case for stock price growth. Celestica will directly benefit from the massive CapEx in AI infrastructure as seen in its topline growth estimates (28% CAGR 2025-2028).

Celestica’s Valuation Leaves No Margin

Celestica's stock price downside risk is in a valuation that appears to be pricing in years of flawless growth. CLS stock’s current metrics indicate that the Street has already credited Celestica for the immense growth outlined in its outlook. This is a situation where the bar for performance is set exceptionally high, and it is leaving little room for operational missteps and market shifts.

Celestica’s forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is 51x against a sector median of 25.8x. Its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 33.7x, more than double the sector median of 15.7x. The forward Price/Book ratio is 15.3x, far above the sector median of 4.8x. These multiples mean that Celestica’s stock is priced at a massive premium relative to its sector peers.

However, as revenue scales from roughly $12 billion in 2025 to over $21 billion by 2027, the forward P/S ratio naturally compresses without requiring multiple expansion. What looks expensive on today’s sales becomes far more reasonable as top-line growth compounds at 30%+ annually.

CLS's stock price does not point to a company waiting for a turnaround, but it reflects a company that is expected to deliver exceptional growth without errors. This priced-for-perfection scenario is a direct risk to the CLS stock price. The above growth story that I detailed is precisely what fuels this high valuation. But because these optimistic forecasts are already at the current stock price of $302, any failure to meet these high expectations may trigger a sharp price correction in CLS stock. A quarterly miss on topline or EPS targets, or any downward revision to outlook, may disproportionately impact CLS stock.

Furthermore, Celestica’s business model adds another layer of risk. Celestica’s profitability, gross profit margin of 12.04% against a sector median of 48.55% is of a business with a heavy manufacturing and integration component where the cost of goods sold is very high. Celestica has improved its operating margin, but the low gross margin indicates limited pricing power on the total value of the products it sells. In a high-valuation view, this is a vulnerability. A small increase in component costs or unexpected pricing pressure from its large hyperscale clients may squeeze profitability, making it harder for Celestica to meet the high earnings expectations.

Finally, Celestica’s growth is highly concentrated on the CapEx cycles of a few large hyperscale and digital native clients. In Q3, three clients accounted for 30%, 15%, and 14% of the total top line. The outlook for 2026 and 2027 is also heavily dependent on the timely ramp-up of massive new programs. Any delay in a client's data center build-out, a shift in a client's technology choice, or a decision to dual-source a previously exclusive program may hit hard on Celestica’s capability to attain its growth targets. Under the current valuations, Street may not forgive such a setback. CLS stock’s 220%+ 1Y price return may not have discounted these concentration and execution risks completely.

Takeaway

Celestica is doing well from an operational standpoint, but it is already baked into sustained and flawless execution. With high multiples, gross margins are tight, and leverage to a few hyperscale cloud clients, the buffer to get by is very small. Just an earnings miss could result in a sharp revaluation.

data.title: Kinsale: A Quality Player In E&S Insurance
data.description:
Thesis

I believe Kinsale Capital (KNSL) is a buy because it is a long-term quality compounder that is consistently profitable and well positioned in a growing E&S market. Their profitability is supported by a significantly low expense ratio, growing their book value and enabling ROE far above their mid-teens target, the industry average, and peers in and above their weight class. Their loss ratios show consistent improvement, while their expense ratios are reducing fast enough to maintain their competitive lead beyond the near term.

Residual income modeling implies that KNSL is currently trading close to intrinsic value with minor overvaluation, using conservative figures. Relative valuation metrics suggest it is overpriced but are notably cheaper compared to their historical averages. Price has fallen by about 13.5% in the past year due to concerns related to premium growth, competition, and the softening market. Given strong financials, compounding book value, and operational efficiency, the price reduction presents an opportunity to enter.

Business Snapshot

Kinsale Capital Group is a mid-sized, pure-play specialty insurance company in the US P&C industry, founded in 2009 and headquartered in Richmond, Virginia. The breakdown of their gross written premiums [GWP] for the first 9 months of 2025 was “70.1% casualty and 29.9% property,” with a focus on small- and medium-sized accounts. They are authorized to sell in "all 50 states, the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands" using their network of insurance brokers.

With 2024 year-end data, we can see that their top 3 states for total premium volume were California (18.8%), Florida (15.6%), and Texas (13.4%). The GWP breakdown by business lines is Commercial (97.4%) and Personal (2.6%). Their top 3 underwriting divisions were all in their commercial lines, and the breakdown by GWP was Commercial property (24.4%), Excess Casualty (13.1%), Small Business Casualty (10.5%). These rankings seem to remain the same for 2025 when looking at quarterly reports, although no specific annual breakdown is provided yet.

Recent Changes

There are concerns related to weakening growth in their Commercial Property division, increasing competition from large standard admitted carriers, and cyclical downturn from a softening market. These reasons are largely responsible for the price decline of about 13.5% in the past year as of Jan 4th. I believe each of these reasons are legitimate concerns but will not undermine its ability to produce above-industry-average excess returns beyond the near term. I plan to cover why they have a structural advantage that enables superior profitability and are a high-quality long-term compounder.

Structurally Supported Profitability

KNSL has structural advantages that enable superior profitability in the E&S industry. Through impressive operational efficiency and their technological platforms, they can maintain strong underwriting profitability across hard and soft markets. For example, on the graph below, we can see their combined ratio is consistently below their peers, as well as the wider P&C industry, by an average of about 18.5 points for the past 10 years. This impressive performance largely stems from their low expense ratios, which come from a level of efficiency that management considers a competitive advantage. Their expense management has been making marginal improvements since 2017. This is enabled by their "proprietary technology platform," which allows them to process a large amount of business without a proportional increase in cost. This works alongside their efficient underwriting and claims adjusting.

An example would be their "freedom of rate and form" by refusing to delegate their underwriting or claims of authority to third parties, which also alleviates costs while ensuring consistent and full control of their claims management and risk selection. Altogether, this gives them the ability to garner excess margins or the leeway to cut rates in a competitive market while still maintaining high profitability. Their structural efficiency also ensures they can sustainably generate value; e.g., for the first nine months of 2025, their annualized ROE was 29.1%, which is notably higher than the industry average of about 10%, their mid-teens target, and their peers.

Combined Ratio: KNSL vs. P&C Industry

Despite this, it can be argued that their superior underwriting profitability may not be durable in the coming years due to the slowing of premium growth and growing competition from standard carriers this past year. If this continues, KNSL's dominant position in the E&S industry may be threatened. To give an example, the growth in their gross written premiums has fallen from 42.3% in 2023 to 6.9% for the first nine months of 2025. This decline, in part, comes from the decline in their largest division, Commercial Property, seeing a reduction of 15.2% in premiums in the first 9 months of 2025 compared to the prior year's period. They've admitted this slowing growth is mainly due to "rate declines and an increasingly competitive environment" as standard carriers enter the market. If these competitive conditions progress, their low expense ratios may be insufficient to maintain their strong margins. Larger carriers, through scale and their diversified books, can take price rate cuts to gain market share from KNSL. All of this would work together to erode KNSL's margins, industry lead, and ability to produce excess returns. Despite ROE being high, we may already be seeing it normalize since their annualized ROE for the first nine months of 2025 decreased to 29.1% from 32.3% in the prior year's period.

However, this argument might be overlooking that KNSLs slowing premium growth in their current largest division is more reflective of their underwriting discipline and strategy rather than a weakening competitive position. Key metrics support that management is prioritizing profitability over volume and selective risk-taking in response to price competition from bigger players entering the E&S market. For example, despite worsening growth in their largest division, their combined ratio for the first nine months of 2024, which was 77.6%, did not get notably worse; for the same period in 2025, it was 77.5%. This shows good stability, as this result was maintained despite declining GWP growth and 2.3 points from net catastrophe losses due to the Palisades Fire, which was more than the 1.7 points of net catastrophe losses in the first nine months of 2024, largely due to hurricanes and tornadoes. We even see improving growth in other areas, e.g., during the first nine months of 2025, excluding their Commercial Property division, growth in GWP had increased by 14.4% compared to the same prior period thanks to their business seeing "strong submission flow from brokers across most divisions." This growth serves as a shield against competitors fighting on price in Commercial Property and highlights another strength of KNSL.

I believe a crucial reason KNSL sees strong submission growth is because of their speed of execution, which is enabled by their tech-based proprietary systems I mentioned earlier. It lets their "underwriters reply to the majority of submissions within 24 hours," allowing brokers to close deals faster. That's in addition to KNSL focusing on small- and medium-sized accounts, which are often overlooked to begin with by larger carriers. Altogether not engaging in a race-to-the-bottom pricing war, they protect their margins while rebalancing their portfolio of policies at prices they find acceptable for the expected risk. In the soft market, this is a better approach than forcing expansion and growth, which can cause adverse selection where they accept low-quality risks to preserve volume, which in the future may cause higher losses and hurt profitability and ROE. This assumes they maintain their underwriting discipline by remaining highly selective, which we've seen over the years since they accept roughly 5-7% of business submissions despite the submissions they receive steadily growing each year.

Quality Compounder

Slowing growth and competition may make us forget that KNSL is still a high-quality compounder in this space due to being a good capital allocator. This has been expressed primarily through underwriting discipline and secondarily through their growing investment income, which has allowed them to generate ROE in the mid-twenties across both soft and hard markets, unlike some peers. For example, I calculated 3-, 5-, and 10-year CAGRs (end period 31st December 2025) for Book Value, Net Premiums Earned, Net Income and Investment Income for KNSL, ACGL, MKL, and RLI. As can be seen in the table below, KNSL consistently ranked highest on these four criteria across those periods of time, which include hard markets (3Y CAGRs) and soft markets (10Y CAGRs). Seeing superior performance across hard and soft markets, we can infer that their returns are more driven by their ability to allocate capital than cyclical tailwinds.

Key Metrics CAGRS

Note: Some MKL 3Y CAGRs had a negative base year, hence the N/AI think the outperformance on these metrics reinforces the existence of structural advantages related to underwriting discipline and structural efficiency, based on their technology and systems. Their rapidly growing investment income suggests they are effectively reinvesting their float, which will allow earnings to multiply even if we see their underwriting growth normalize in the current market. We see this reflected in the growth of their net income and book value, and this is only strengthened by living in a healthy and growing E&S market. I believe all of these imply long-run price appreciation because for insurers, their intrinsic value is largely driven by book value compounding at high ROE across cycles, which we see here.

Underwriting Performance

I'd like to compare KNSL with peers to showcase how competitive they are in terms of their underwriting ratios. I'll compare them with other specialty insurers of varying sizes, such as ACGL, RLI, and MKL, to showcase KNSL's dominance. The combined ratio is the sum of their loss ratio and expense ratio. I'd like to focus on their combined ratio and expense ratios since their loss ratios are competitive but not enough to be a difference maker among their peers. They boast industry-leading combined ratios resulting in substantial margins. These are mostly due to their low expense ratio, which is persistently below their peers and the P&C industry.

As seen on the combined ratio graph below, KNSL frequently demonstrates a lower combined ratio than its peers. For example, on the graph below, since 2021 they have maintained a sub-80% combined ratio, and the persistent gap over the period 2013-2025 highlights the existence of their competitive advantage. This has remained true across soft and hard markets despite larger peers exerting scale-related advantages. This highlights that KNSL's advantage is more process-driven than scale-driven, suggesting they are not necessarily at a disadvantage if larger peers continue to enter the E&S market. Having stronger margins than others corroborates the compounding of their book value and mid-twenties ROE.

They have also been seeing steady improvements in margin since 2020 along with peers, but lately, we can see since 2024 KNSL, along with some peers, has remained flat or increased, likely due to softening market conditions. Since long-term insurer returns are strongly driven by underwriting margin, seeing that they can outperform peers by a consistent margin across different markets supports the fact that they are a high-quality long-term compounder. Just for a bit of clarification, the reason for their massive dip in 2015 was due to their reliance on a multi-line quota share (MLQS) reinsurance agreement where they ceded a large portion of their risk to reinsurers in exchange for large chunks of their GWPs. This had the effect of drastically lowering their loss and expense ratios in the years before 2016. This ended in 2016 after their IPO; hence their combined ratio normalized to the 75-85% range.

Combined Ratio: Peer Comparison

KNSL has a structural cost advantage due to their tech-based systems that enable low expense ratios. The graph below shows us that even after they ended their MLQS reinsurance agreement, their expense ratio normalized to about 25% in 2017. This was below the industry's expense ratio of 27% in 2017. Their annualized expense ratio for 2025 is 20.56%, which is below the industry's 25.2% as of mid-year 2025 and also meaningfully below their peers. Since 2017, they've maintained this advantage with incremental gains. Recently peers have experienced higher expense ratios alongside a softening market. Despite sharing the same conditions, KNSL has remained stable. Their margins and low costs allow them to compete on price at levels some peers would find difficult to maintain for extended periods of time, in exchange for sacrificing margin. As they grow, their lead may widen due to size-related advantages, strengthening the case for their expenses not rising in proportion to their GWP due to their cost advantage. This shows how KNSL can maintain superior underwriting margins and manage their mid-20s ROE across market cycles, even as their premiums compound at rates greater than competitors, again reinforcing why they are a high-quality insurer among their peers.

Expense Ratio: Peer Comparison

Valuation

Multi-Stage Residual Income Model

A multi-stage residual income model (RIM) suggests that the mean intrinsic value per share of KNSL is $379.80. The stock price as of the 12th of Jan 2026 is $397, suggesting the stock is modestly overvalued at 4.3%, leaving them close to fair value. This presents investors with a chance to build or add to a position in a wonderful company at a roughly fair price, given its fundamentals.

Estimated Intrinsic Value Range

The intrinsic value per share is the sum of the current book value per share, the present value of residual income, and the present value of the terminal value. The residual income is the difference between net income and the minimum required return an investor expects for each forecast year. The terminal value is the sum of residual income earned in perpetuity from year 15 onwards. The book value per share is the assets minus liabilities, i.e., shareholders' equity, on a per-share basis.

Composition of Intrinsic Value:

Components

Book value per share

An investor's required minimum return, aka cost of equity, is calculated using the CAPM formula, Ra=Rrf+[Ba x (Rm-Rrf)]. This formula uses the risk-free rate, equity risk premium, and the beta. The risk-free rate used is the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.175% as of Jan 2026. The US equity risk premium of 5, as of Sep 2025, is the extra return an investor expects for accepting the risks of equities over the risk-free asset. The beta of 1 tells us how volatile a stock is relative to the market. Greater than one means it is more volatile than the market and vice versa. If it is 1, then it moves in perfect sync with the market on average. Inputting these values gives me the cost of equity of 9.18%, which will be used to discount the residual income and terminal value to get their present values.

CAPM Inputs

I chose a persistence factor of 0.59; this is the decay rate of the excess returns. It corresponds to a narrow and stable moat, which I think reflects their underwriting discipline and operational efficiency in their space. The persistence factor does not have a significant effect on the intrinsic value. I set the dividend payout ratio to half in 5 years from now, which would be 1.89%; it also doesn't have a critical impact on the intrinsic value. Their dividend yield is not an attractive part of this investment since its 4-year average dividend yield is 0.16%, much lower than the sector median of 3.35%.

Assumptions Table:
